description: CHW-level protocols for diabetes and hypertension self-management: reading numbers, medication adherence, low-cost monitoring, diet on a budget, when to go to the ER. The two conditions that dominate underserved-community morbidity.
source: ADA Standards of Care 2024, JNC 8 hypertension guidelines, CDC National Diabetes Education Program, American Heart Association, National Kidney Foundation

triples.subject: HbA1c (A1c)
triples.object: average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months — expressed as a percentage of hemoglobin that has glucose attached

triples.subject: HbA1c targets
triples.object: below 5.7% — no diabetes

triples.subject: HbA1c targets
triples.object: 5.7%–6.4% — act now with lifestyle change; metformin is an option for high-risk individuals

triples.subject: HbA1c targets
triples.object: below 7.0% for most adults with diabetes (ADA 2024); below 8.0% acceptable for elderly or those with hypoglycemia risk

triples.subject: HbA1c
triples.object: above 10% — indicates very poor control; warrants same-week provider contact, not waiting for next scheduled visit

triples.subject: blood glucose (daily readings)
triples.object: 80–130 mg/dL before meals (ADA goal for most adults on diabetes medications)

triples.subject: blood glucose (daily readings)
triples.object: below 180 mg/dL one to two hours after eating

triples.subject: blood glucose
triples.object: below 70 mg/dL is hypoglycemia — treat immediately with 15g fast carbs (4 oz juice, 3-4 glucose tabs, 1 tbsp honey)

triples.subject: blood glucose
triples.object: above 400 mg/dL — call provider immediately; above 600 mg/dL with confusion or vomiting is a 911 emergency (hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state)

triples.subject: blood pressure
triples.object: below 120/80 mmHg

triples.subject: blood pressure
triples.object: 130–139/80–89 mmHg — lifestyle change; medication if cardiovascular risk is high

triples.subject: blood pressure
triples.object: 140+/90+ mmHg — medication indicated alongside lifestyle change

triples.subject: blood pressure
triples.object: above 180/120 mmHg without symptoms — call provider same day; do not drive yourself to ER unless symptomatic

triples.subject: blood pressure
triples.object: above 180/120 mmHg WITH chest pain, severe headache, vision changes, facial droop, arm weakness, or difficulty speaking — call 911 immediately

triples.subject: diabetes medication adherence
triples.object: cost — metformin is generic and typically under $10/month at Walmart, Costco, or Cost Plus Drugs; always ask about generic options

triples.subject: diabetes medication adherence
triples.object: OTC human insulin (ReliOn brand at Walmart) is available without prescription for ~$25/vial; not identical to analog insulins but functional for many type 2 patients — discuss with provider

triples.subject: diabetes medication adherence
triples.object: if a dose is missed, take it as soon as remembered unless it is almost time for the next — never double-dose; always carry a list of medications

triples.subject: hypertension medication adherence
triples.object: side effects — ACE inhibitors can cause dry cough (ARBs are alternative); diuretics cause frequent urination; beta-blockers cause fatigue — all are manageable with provider help

triples.subject: hypertension medication adherence
triples.object: stopping beta-blockers suddenly can trigger rebound hypertension or angina — always taper under provider guidance

triples.subject: low-cost blood glucose monitoring
triples.object: ReliOn Premier meter and strips at Walmart — strips ~$9/50 count; no prescription needed; compatible with most Medicaid formularies

triples.subject: low-cost blood pressure monitoring
triples.object: upper-arm automatic cuffs validated by American Medical Association available under $40 (Omron HEM-7120 series); wrist cuffs are less accurate

triples.subject: low-cost blood pressure monitoring
triples.object: many pharmacies (CVS, Rite Aid, Walmart) have free blood pressure kiosks — sit quietly for 5 minutes first, take 2 readings 1 minute apart and average them

triples.subject: diabetes diet on a food budget
triples.object: control carbohydrate portion sizes, not total elimination — choose high-fiber slow-digesting carbs over refined ones

triples.subject: diabetes diet on a food budget
triples.object: dried beans and lentils (lowest glycemic index legumes, under $1/lb), canned sardines, eggs, frozen vegetables, oats (plain rolled), brown rice in small portions

triples.subject: diabetes diet on a food budget
triples.object: sugar-sweetened beverages are the single most impactful dietary change — swap soda for water; fruit juice spikes blood sugar as fast as soda

triples.subject: hypertension diet on a food budget
triples.object: reduce sodium to under 1,500mg/day; increase potassium (bananas, canned tomatoes, beans, potatoes with skin) — potassium blunts sodium's blood pressure effect

triples.subject: hypertension diet on a food budget
triples.object: canned goods have high sodium — rinse canned beans and vegetables under water to remove up to 40% of sodium; choose 'no salt added' when available

triples.subject: diabetes and hypertension
triples.object: occur together in 70% of people with type 2 diabetes — managing both simultaneously is critical; uncontrolled hypertension accelerates diabetic kidney damage

triples.subject: diabetic complications
triples.object: any break in skin on the foot, even a small cut or blister, requires daily inspection and provider contact within 48 hours if not healing — diabetic foot infections can progress to amputation in days

triples.subject: when to go to ER
triples.object: blood sugar above 400 with symptoms (nausea, vomiting, confusion, rapid breathing), fruity breath odor (DKA), blood sugar cannot be raised above 70 mg/dL after two attempts with fast carbs

triples.subject: when to go to ER
triples.object: BP above 180/120 with chest pain, severe headache, vision changes, neurological symptoms, or difficulty breathing — these are hypertensive emergency signs requiring IV medication
